focus: RQ5: contract-generated asset, presentation, mode/default, and execution-target tables; router-thinning thresholds; ownership-label prevention
noveltyJustification: 4 of 5 findings were fully new contract, generation, or threshold details and 1 converted the known label defect into a prevention design.
ruledOut: Hand-authoring generated tables and comparing them later
LOC-only hard failures for fat routers
Using compiled executor contracts as router source

title: One normalized record generates four correlated sections
summary: Assets, presentation surfaces, mode/default policy, and execution targets are projections of one versioned command record.

title: Generated sections preserve authored router gates
summary: Stable generated markers replace contract-derived sections while frontmatter, gates, bindings, and short summaries remain authored.

title: Ownership triggers extraction; size signals triage
summary: Presentation or workflow leakage is a hard move trigger; 120 total lines, assets after line 60, and more than two manual router subsections are soft audit signals.

title: Role and media type generate ownership labels
summary: Typed role/media metadata prevents .txt presentation assets from being described as Markdown and enables extension compatibility checks.

title: The deep compiler should consume the shared record
summary: Existing compiled executor contracts provide a typed-generation seam but remain derived consumers rather than router-table authority.

text: A declared mode must resolve through one record to an existing target asset and one generated execution-target row.
A presentation asset path, media type, ownership label, and presentation-boundary reference must be generated from the same asset record.

reason: Long mandatory-input gates can be owner-correct; size is a migration signal, not the ownership boundary.
